headline: Lewis and Clark Reach Mouth of Judith River, 1805
body:
The morning of May twenty-ninth, eighteen hundred and five, dawned upon the Lewis and Clark Expedition with the Missouri River still unfurling its grand, serpentine path through the vast, untamed wilderness. The previous night had been one of wild alarm, a testament to the raw, untamed spirit of this land. A great bull buffalo, driven by some primal instinct, had swum the broad river, clambered over a perogue, and charged through the sleeping encampment, a thunderous, unthinking force of nature. It was a moment of stark, thrilling reality, a reminder of the thin veil between the explorers and the boundless, unpredictable forces of the wild. Yet, as the sun climbed, painting the eastern sky with hues of nascent light, the expedition pressed onward, propelled by an insatiable curiosity for the unfolding landscape. 

It was then, at a distance of two and a half miles from their last camp, that a new marvel revealed itself: a handsome river, discharging its clear waters into the Missouri from the larboard side. Captain Lewis, ever the keen observer of nature's intricate designs, disembarked and ascended this newfound tributary for a mile and a half, his spirit undoubtedly stirred by the fresh discovery. He found it a hundred yards wide from bank to bank, with seventy-five yards of water flowing over a bed of gravel and mud, remarkably free of obstructing rocks. Its current, though rapid, promised navigability, a potential artery into the heart of the continent. But it was the water itself that captivated, for as Lewis noted in his journal, it was "clearer much than any we have met with." This clarity spoke of distant, pristine origins, of mountain springs and untouched alpine meadows, a stark contrast to the often-turbid Missouri. 

The banks of this river, though low, bore the marks of infrequent overflow, suggesting a gentle, consistent flow. The surrounding high country, through which this river carved its course, teemed with the "great abundance of the Argalia or Bighorned animals," their majestic forms a living testament to the richness of this wild domain. Captain Clark, having ascended the river even further, bestowed upon it a name, honoring Julia Hancock, his future wife, a personal touch woven into the grand tapestry of exploration. The bottoms of this stream, as far as the eye could discern, were wider and more heavily timbered than those of the Missouri, boasting a verdant intermixture of box elder, cottonwood, willow, rose bushes, honeysuckle, and red willow, a thriving undergrowth providing sustenance and shelter for countless creatures. 

Evidence of human presence, too, was etched into the landscape. Just above the confluence with the Missouri, Captain Lewis counted the remains of one hundred and twenty-six Indian lodges, their fires extinguished perhaps a fortnight prior. Captain Clark, on the opposite bank, observed an even older encampment. The Indian woman, Sacagawea, with her intimate knowledge of these lands and their peoples, examined the moccasins found at these sites, discerning that they belonged not to her own Shoshone nation, but to a people who inhabited the country north of the Missouri and west of the Rocky Mountains, likely the Minetaries of Fort de Prairie. 

Further along their journey that day, the expedition encountered a grim spectacle, a stark reminder of the harsh realities of survival in this land. They passed a precipice, one hundred and twenty feet high, where Indians had driven vast herds of buffalo to their demise. The mangled carcasses, numbering at least a hundred, created a "most horrid stench," a testament to the brutal efficiency of this ancient hunting method. Wolves, fat and unperturbed by human presence, feasted on the remains, a natural cycle of life and death playing out in the grand theater of the wilderness. The day, though marked by discovery and wonder, also bore witness to the raw, unvarnished struggle for existence, a profound duality that defines the very essence of the wild. The clear waters of the Judith, the teeming wildlife, the ancient hunting grounds, all whispered tales of a land both bountiful and unforgiving, a wilderness waiting to be understood.
location: Mouth of Judith River, Fergus County, Montana
